sections.heading: The core distinction
sections.body: Countable nouns can be counted as individual units and have both singular and plural forms: one apple, two apples. Uncountable nouns (also called mass nouns) refer to things seen as a continuous mass or concept — they have no natural plural and take singular verbs.

sections.heading: Common uncountable nouns that surprise learners
sections.body: Many nouns that have natural equivalents in Spanish are uncountable in English: advice (not 'advices'), information (not 'informations'), furniture (not 'furnitures'), luggage, homework, research, news, weather, traffic, money, time, work, knowledge.

sections.heading: Quantifiers
sections.body: Use different quantifiers depending on count status. Countable: many, few, a few, a number of, several. Uncountable: much, little, a little, a great deal of, an amount of. Both: some, any, a lot of, plenty of, most.

sections.heading: Making uncountable nouns countable
sections.body: You can make an uncountable noun countable by adding a container or unit: 'a piece of advice', 'a piece of information', 'two items of furniture', 'three pieces of luggage', 'a bit of research'. This is how native speakers quantify them.

examples.english: Can you give me some advice?
There's a lot of traffic today.

exercises.instruction: Are these nouns countable (C) or uncountable (U)? Then use them in a short sentence.
answers: U, C, U, C, U, C, U, C

quiz.question: Which sentence is correct?
options: She gave me a good advice.
She gave me some good advices.
She gave me a good piece of advice.
quiz.explanation: 'Advice' is uncountable. To refer to one item, use 'a piece of advice'.

quiz.question: Which quantifier can be used with BOTH countable and uncountable nouns?
quiz.explanation: 'A lot of' works with both: 'a lot of apples' (C) and 'a lot of water' (U).
